entry.title: SAP Business Network for Logistics 2602 – What’s New?
entry.content:
The latest release for SAP Business Network for Logistics delivers a broad set of improvements designed to make scheduling, data management, and cross-center collaboration more reliable and efficient. While several features add valuable capabilities, the highlight of this release is the introduction of recurring blocks for loading points, which unlocks powerful, predictable planning that reduces manual effort and last-minute changes.

Below is a concise tour of what’s new, who it helps, and practical takeaways to start realizing benefits quickly. And at the end, there is an invitation to a ‘Features in Focus’ webinar for shippers and carriers on SAP Business Network for Logistics. Check it out! It’s for free!

SAP Business Network Freight Collaboration

‘Administration Guides for Logistics Service Providers’ and ‘Activate your Account’ guides: language versions to be created manually

The process of downloading PDF versions of translated guides has changed.

Now, you need to manually create PDF language versions of the ’Administration Guide for Logistics Providers’ and ’Activate Your Account’ guides.

To do so, create the translated version on SAP Help Portal and then choose ‘PDF - Create Custom PDF’.

 

Dock Appointment Scheduling: Define daily, weekly or monthly recurring blockers

Recurring blocks allow loading-point time blocks to be defined with daily, weekly, or monthly recurrence. This means you can plan for regular occurrences such as holidays, maintenance windows, or cleaning downtime without recreating blocks each period.

When creating a new recurring block, the system checks for conflicts with existing appointments or blocks to prevent overbooking or double-booking.

Note that recurring blocks cannot be imported from or exported to Microsoft Excel, so planning should be managed within the ‘Manage Entities’ application. The practical benefit is a predictable, automated scheduling rhythm that reduces manual updates and minimizes scheduling surprises during peak periods or special events.

 

For more information, please refer to our additional feature detail deck and SAP Community blog post.

Dock Appointment Scheduling: Define blocks related to max capacity

Previously, users had to manually specify the blocked capacity, and overbooking limits could not be included. With the updated time block feature, shippers can now create blocks that apply to the maximum capacity of a loading point, including overbooking in the ‘Manage Entities’ app. The system checks available capacity when creating time blocks and alerts users if total capacity is exceeded to resolve potential capacity clashes.

 

 This feature helps drive higher utilization of loading-point capacity while keeping service levels intact during peak or constrained periods.

More information on this feature can be found in this SAP Community blog post.

Dock Appointment Scheduling: Microsoft Excel upload / download of loading point data now also supports the fields

The ‘Manage Entities’ app now supports additional fields in Microsoft Excel uploads and downloads. You can include:

  • ‘Use Derived Appointment Duration’ (on a loading point)
  • ‘Max. Capacity’ (on a loading point – block time)
  • ‘Include Overbooking’ (on a loading point – block time)

Dock Appointment Scheduling: based on location defined in TM, loading point can be predefined and cannot be changed

The loading point ID and unloading point ID can be passed from Transportation Management to SAP Business Network for Logistics. This data travels via the ‘Location’ field under ‘Additional Address’ (Source or Destination) in TM.

When the internal ID matches an existing loading point ID, the system preselects and fixes that loading point, preventing changes by shippers or carriers in SAP the ‘Manage Dock Appointments’ and ‘Self-Book Dock Appointments’ apps.

 

Dock Appointment Scheduling: Enhanced appointment validation

The GenericAppointment_In API now triggers an advanced validation of available time blocks and capacity.

If there is no existing time block for the requested slot and enough capacity is available, the system automatically confirms the booking and returns a GenericAppointment_Out with status code '03'.

Conversely, if a time block exists or capacity is insufficient, the system does not confirm the appointment and returns a GenericAppointment_Out with detailed error information.

This gives users clear, actionable feedback and a faster path to a confirmed slot when possible, while maintaining strict accuracy and transparency when issues arise.

 

SAP Business Network Freight Collaboration and SAP Business Network Global Track and Trace

Milestone Reporting: ‘Manage Freight Execution’ app: now supporting cross-data center collaboration

The Manage Freight Execution app now supports cross data center connections, enabling visibility of freight orders and events across data centers. This means shippers’ orders and events are visible to carriers on other data centers, and vice versa, improving collaboration and timeliness of information.

Milestone Reporting: ‘Manage Freight Execution’ app: new filters available on ‘Arrival State’ and ‘Departure State

New filters in ‘Manage Freight Execution’ let users refine searches by ‘Arrival State’ and ‘Departure State’, enabling more precise document discovery based on location status and timing. The benefits are clearer, faster insight and improved cross-organizational coordination.

 

SAP Business Network Freight Collaboration and SAP Business Network, intelligent insights add-on

Harmonization of predefined analytics content

The predefined analytics content for SAP Business Network Freight Collaboration was previously available as

  • Predefined stories in the ‘Manage Analytic Stories’ app for use with embedded analytics
  • Business contents that can be imported into your SAP Analytics Cloud tenant

To reduce duplication between the two, as of November 15, 2025, the business content was already deprecated, and you can't import them into SAP Analytics Cloud. With this release, you can’t display the imported business content in SAP Analytics Cloud dashboards. Instead, you can use story dashboards within intelligent insights that integrates embedded analytics toolsets for data analysis.

This shift reduces duplication and aligns analytics with the embedded analytics approach.

 

Announcement for shippers and carriers on SAP Business Network for Logistics

Join our upcoming webinar to discover how both shippers and carriers can seamlessly integrate, collaborate, and optimize their end-to-end logistics processes using REST APIs. Don’t miss this opportunity to unlock the full power of SAP Business Network for Logistics!

This session will be held on March 4th, 2026. It is expected to last approximately 45 minutes and will also be recorded.

To secure your spot for this informative webinar, please visit the below registration link, according to your region. Once registered, and you are unable to attend, you will receive the recording of the webinar to enjoy in your own time. 

Choose the time that works best for you (same content in both sessions): 

Click here to register for Option 1 on March 4th 11:00am IST / 2:30pm JST / 4:30pm AEDT

Click here to register for Option 2 on March 4th 11:00am EST / 1:00pm BRT / 4:00pm GMT

 

--------------------

The above summarizes the key innovations for 2602. We hope you enjoy the new capabilities. Thanks for reading the blog post and stay tuned! 

A full overview of the 2602 release can be found under the following link.

To learn more about SAP Business Network for Logistics visit SAP - Business Network for Logistics.

For further questions and feedback related to the blog, please check the Q&A area and feel free to post your questions in the link with the tag SAP Business Network for Logistics.

entry.title: SAP BI 2025: Why RichText is Richer Than You Think
entry.content:
The SAP BusinessObjects BI 2025 release introduces the RichText interactive element, a powerful new addition to Web Intelligence that transforms how you present textual content in your reports.

What Can You Do with RichText?

🎨Effortless Formatting

Design text to display like articles or enriched presentation. The intuitive toolbar gives you quick access to essential styling options: fonts, colors, alignments, and bullet lists—all without any code knowledge.

  • Typography controls – fonts, sizes, and weights
  • Visual styling – colors, highlights, and emphasis
  • Layout options – alignments, bullet lists, and spacing
  • Paste – External HTML content can be imported. All content is sanitized for security purposes.

 

Dynamic Data Integration

RichText seamlessly integrates with your business data through Dynamic Data Objects. Simply drag and drop objects into the RichText feeding pane, just as you would with any other report element.

To insert dynamic data:

  1. Click the Insert Objects button
  2. Select your desired business object
  3. Customize the display format to match your needs

Quick Examples:

A single aggregated value as KPI:


Handling multiple values? Navigate to the Multiple Values section in settings, where you can define custom separators—semicolons, commas, or even HTML tags—to format dynamic lists exactly as you envision them.





Similar example using HTML separator:

 

 

 


 

The Hidden Power of RichText

RichText delivers far more than basic text formatting. Combine live data with HTML5 capabilities, and your creativity becomes the only boundary.

🌐 HTML & CSS Integration

Leverage modern web standards to create advanced layouts and custom styling that bring contemporary design directly into Web Intelligence.

Real-world example: Imagine adding a dynamic, context-sensitive tooltip at the top of your report that provides rich details on-demand—without cluttering your tables or charts.

In the video example, mouse hover triggers tooltips displaying live data:

 

How to Create such Interactive Tooltips?

  1. Add the RichText Element Drop a new RichText element onto your report canvas.
  2. Feed Your Data Add at least one dimension as an object in the Feeding pane, or drag it directly onto the element in the canvas.
    • In this example, we're using the [Lines] dimension.
  •  Switch to HTML Mode In the RichText-specific settings pane, switch to HTML mode and remove the default separator character—leave it blank.

  • Insert the HTML Code Paste this HTML snippet:
<div class="hover-wrap">Show Top 5 Products<div class="table-column"><div class="table-head">Best Products</div>{[Lines]}</div></div>
  • Access Advanced Settings Select the fifth tab labeled "Advanced Settings."
  • Add the CSS Styling Paste the following CSS code:
overflow: visible;
cursor: default;

.hover-wrap {
  padding: 6px 10px;
  border: 1px solid #d0d7e2;
  border-radius: 8px;
  position: relative;
  display: inline-block;
  overflow: visible;
}

.table-column {
  position: absolute;
  left: 0;
  top: calc(100% + 6px);
  width: auto;
  min-width: 220px;
  max-width: min(90vw, 360px);
  z-index: 9999;
  border: 1px solid #d7dce3;
  border-radius: 10px;
  background: #fff;
  box-shadow: 0 10px 28px rgba(1, 23, 42, 0.2);
  overflow: visible;
  opacity: 0;
  transform: translateY(8px);
  pointer-events: none;
  transition: opacity 180ms ease, transform 180ms ease;
  margin-top: 0;
}

.hover-wrap:hover .table-column {
  opacity: 1;
  transform: translateY(0);
}

.table-head {
  padding: 6px 10px;
  border-top-left-radius: 10px;
  border-top-right-radius: 10px;
  background: linear-gradient(135deg, #2563eb, #3b82f6);
  color: #fff;
  font-weight: 600;
}

.table-column span {
  display: block;
  padding: 4px 8px;
  border-bottom: 1px solid #e5e9f0;
  background: #f9fafb;
  white-space: normal;
  word-break: break-word;
}

.table-column span:nth-child(even) {
  background: #f2f5fb;
}

.table-column span:last-child {
  border-bottom: none;
}

 

Test Your Tooltip Hover your mouse over the RichText element in the canvas. You should now see a beautifully decorated tooltip appear on screen with your live data!

 

Ready to Explore?

The RichText interactive element is available now in SAP BusinessObjects Web Intelligence 2025. Give it a try and see how it transforms your reporting experience.

RichText's HTML and CSS integration opens up endless possibilities for interactive reporting in SAP BI. Interactive tooltips are merely the starting point. The real magic happens when you combine your creativity with live data to transform static reports into engaging, dynamic experiences for consumers.

entry.title: Unlocking Strategic Pricing with SAP Advanced Commodity Pricing Engine
entry.content:
In today’s global markets, commodities are no longer just raw materials—they’re strategic assets. Volatile prices, complex contracts, and multiple market indices make commodity management a high-stakes challenge for companies in energy, agriculture, metals and manufacturing. Enter SAP Advanced Commodity Pricing Engine a core component of SAP Commodity Management, providing a powerful tool designed to simplify, automate, and optimize commodity pricing.

What is SAP Advanced Commodity Pricing Engine?

The Advanced Commodity Pricing Engine is a sophisticated module in SAP that enables companies to calculate commodity prices dynamically based on market indices, contracts, and predefined rules. Unlike static pricing methods, Advanced Commodity Pricing Engine can handle complex pricing formulas, variable discounts, secondary cost adjustments, and quality differentials, giving businesses a single source of truth for commodity costs.

Key highlights include:

  • Automated price calculations based on market benchmarks
  • Integration with SAP S/4HANA for seamless procurement, sales, and finance processes
  • Flexibility to handle diverse commodities like crude oil, refined fuel products, metals, grains, and chemical products
  • Drives commodity risk exposure to facilitate risk management and mitigation

Why Businesses Need Advanced Commodity Pricing Engine

Commodity price fluctuations are unpredictable. Companies that rely on manual pricing processes face several challenges:

  • Risk of errors and inconsistencies across regions
  • Delays in price updates, leading to financial losses
  • Difficulty in tracking contract obligations against market movements

Advanced Commodity Pricing Engine addresses these challenges by:

  1. Standardizing pricing logic across all commodities
  2. Reducing operational complexity with automated calculations
  3. Enabling proactive risk management by linking prices to market indices
  4. Enhancing transparency for audit and compliance purposes

Core Features of Advanced Commodity Pricing Engine

  1. Dynamic Price Formulas
    Configure formulas that automatically calculate prices using market indices, secondary charges, and quality adjustments.
  2. Index-Based Pricing
    Link your contracts to global benchmarks (e.g., Brent crude, LME metals, CME wheat futures) for real-time market-reflective pricing.
  3. Flexible Adjustments
    Incorporate location-specific logistics, quality differentials, and taxes into price calculations automatically.
  4. Scenario Simulation
    Run “what-if” scenarios to assess the impact of price fluctuations on margins and contracts before they occur.  
  5. Seamless Integration
    Works with SAP S/4HANA’s procurement, sales, finance, and logistics modules, ensuring a unified view of commodity costs across the e2e supply chain.

What’s new with Advanced Commodity Pricing Engine

The new Advanced Commodity Pricing Engine offers the following enhancements:

  • A new RAP business object R_CmmdtyPrcgConditionFormulaTP for formula data model and a new RAP business object R_CmmdtyPrcgPriceFixationSetTP for price fixation groups of future basis pricing process have been included.
  • A new CPE interface CL_CMM_CPE_INTERFACE_BO allows you to update CPE data in batch scenarios through BAPI.
  • Changes to the allocation data can be made manually through the allocation workbench (CMM_ALLOC). With this functionality, you will now be able to switch the mode from automatic allocation to manual.
  • Price quotations are now visible in display mode for logistic documents. This allows you to revisit the quotations from the last time it was updated.
  • All the pricing aspects in logistic documents are now saved locally within the document. With this functionality, the changes are isolated to the specific document.
  • Optimized Future/Basis Pricing scenarios.
  • Considerable performance improvement for mass processing scenarios with high data volume.

Benefits of Implementing Advanced Commodity Pricing Engine

  • New strategic engine: where all new/enhanced capabilities and innovations would be incorporated in the future
  • Enhanced Transactional data consistency: Configuration changes in formula do not impact existing contracts using defaulted formula details.
  • Operational Efficiency: Automates complex pricing, reducing manual intervention and errors.
  • Financial Accuracy: Real-time market pricing ensures accurate P&L reporting and cost allocation.
  • Strategic Advantage: Quickly adapt to market changes, optimizing procurement, hedging, and sales strategies.
  • Regulatory Compliance: Transparent pricing formulas and audit trails help meet internal and external reporting requirements.
  • Clean Core: Standard extensibility options such as key user and developer extensibility is supported with the introduction of the RAP (ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model) data model.
  • Cloud APIs: RAP re-use component offers cloud consumable APIs.
  • Increased end user satisfaction: due to enhanced performance and system response time.

Who Should Use Advanced Commodity Pricing Engine?

Companies dealing with:

  • Energy Commodities: Oil, refined products, biofuels
  • Agriculture & Consumer Products: Grains, cocoa, coffee, sugar
  • Metals & Mining: Base metals, precious metals, concentrates, coal
  • Chemicals & Raw Materials: Fertilizers, polymers, specialty chemicals

If your business deals with high-volume commodities, dynamic market conditions, or complex contracts, Advanced Commodity Pricing Engine can transform the way you manage pricing.

Conclusion - a powerful tool designed to simplify, automate, and optimize commodity pricing.

In volatile commodity markets, price accuracy isn’t just a convenience—it’s a competitive advantage. SAP Advanced Commodity Pricing Engine empowers organizations to calculate, automate, and optimize commodity prices while integrating seamlessly into existing SAP workflows.

By adopting Advanced Commodity Pricing Engine, businesses can reduce operational risk, improve financial transparency, and stay agile in the face of market fluctuations—turning commodity pricing from a challenge into a strategic tool.

For more information please follow this link to help.sap.com: help.sap.com - Advanced Commodity Pricing Engine

Stay tuned for future innovations by following SAP Roadmap Explorer  for Commodity Management: https://roadmaps.sap.com/board?PRODUCT=42F2E964F4E71EDAA6A3587248CD40E5&range=CURRENT-LAST

entry.content:
There is a requirement from the business to open 2L, 3L, and P2 ledgers only (not 0L) for specific company codes. The configuration Assign Fiscal Year Variants to Ledgers and Company Codes allow saving of different PPV (with different ledgers) for one company code. Please confirm if this setup is feasible.


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Open Posting Periods for Specific Ledgers Only
entry.content:
See help documentation on Alternative Fiscal Year Variant

See also community blog 

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blog-posts-by-sap/in-depth-with-sap-s-4hana-ledger-scoping-q-amp-a/ba-p/13571167

In particular see 

Question 38:
Can I use the fiscal year variant to close periods differently for each ledger?
Answer:
Yes, it is recommended to define a different posting period variant if an alternative fiscal year variant (AFYV) is used.
Note that if an AFYV is assigned to the non-leading ledger, you need a different posting period. Opening and closing posting periods is defined in the posting period variants and can differ between leading and non-leading ledger.

Question 39:
Can I have different closing periods for the leading ledger and the non-leading ledger? For example, to close the non-leading ledger first and leave only the leading ledger open for additional entries such as GAAP conversion or audit adjustment.
Answer:
Adjustments are recorded for the non-leading ledger. All postings in the system are recorded for leading and non-leading ledger.
So, closing the non-leading ledger will not really have an impact for the leading ledger.
If you have an alternative fiscal year variant assigned to the non-leading ledger, you should define a corresponding posting period variant.
This will allow you to close the periods separately in the leading ledger and the non-leading ledger.

See also Posting Periods

entry.title: Custom Cube for Margin Analysis - Calculated Key Figure shows no data
entry.content:
Hello Experts,

i posted this question initially in another Q&A Forum because i was not sure if this Learning Group is the right place to ask questions.

But since i recieved no answer to my question i will post the question in this Learning Group again.

Some background information:

Back in R/3 we used KE35 (Form) and KER1 (Key Figure Scheme) to prepare for KE30 (Profitability Report) in SAP CO-PA modul.

So far i have done significant tests only with transient view entity … analytical query and was able to replace our custom reports from R/3 costing based COPA to S/4 Margin Analysis queries. The results are very good and V3 consumption views are nice to use.

However, i noticed that using a custom consumption view offers very little reuseability. A new requirement triggers a new consumption view and copy/paste of the CDS view. So i want to achieve REUSEABILITY for restricted measures and calculated measures. Looking at the SAP VDM i think it is time to build custom cubes, which i successfully circumvented so far.

So how can i replace R/3 „Forms“, „Key Figures Schemes“ and „Reports“ with Embedded Analytics? I am trying to do a crossover to the VDM which uses cubes, querys, restricted measures, and calculated measures. (Screenshot shows R/3 setup of CO-PA reporting)

I am not trying to replicate the old world 1 by 1, but take over what is still useful e.g. „Prepare a Form“ and „Prepare Key Figures“ and reuse this in multiple querys.

I created the above „custom VDM“ and while the values of the restricted measures are shown correct (see below, RSRT) in the consumption view, the calcuated measures are always inital. What am i doing wrong?

Formulas are very simple:

Revenue - COGS = ContributionMargin1 and FIDirect - OverhAlloc = FIMinusOverhead

My guess is that the calculated measures may have lost their dimensions? Could that be the reason?

Or do i not understand how aggregation works?

Greatful for any tipps. I can share the CDS Views if needed.

Thank you

Regards

David

entry.title: Re: Guidance on best adapter choice for inbound integrati...
entry.content:
Hello,

Thank you very much for your question. I'm very sorry for the delay.

In this case, I asked one of your internal chatbots for support. It referred to the following information sources:

SAP S/4HANA Cloud ABAP Pipeline vs Cloud Data Integration

Extract Data from SAP S/4HANA Cloud and Integrate

Here's the response. I hope that it helps:

When considering inbound integration scenarios using SAP Cloud Integration, especially for creating and updating business objects in SAP Cloud Private, several adapter options are available. Here is a breakdown of the most commonly recommended adapters and considerations for each:

1. OData Services

  • Advantages: OData services are standardized, REST-based, and support CRUD operations, making them ideal for integration with web or mobile applications. They are particularly suitable if your backend is built using modern paradigms such as RAP/CDS, as OData naturally aligns with these technologies.
  • Considerations: OData is recommended when you need a flexible, scalable solution that can easily integrate with various front-end technologies.

2. IDoc

  • Advantages: IDocs are robust and well-suited for batch processing and large data volumes. They are commonly used in SAP-to-SAP integrations.
  • Considerations: IDocs might not be the best choice for real-time or synchronous operations due to their asynchronous nature.

3. RFC/BAPI

  • Advantages: RFCs and BAPIs are powerful for direct SAP system interactions and can handle complex business logic.
  • Considerations: These are more suitable for SAP-to-SAP integrations and might require more effort to expose to non-SAP systems.

4. SOAP

  • Advantages: SOAP is a well-established protocol that supports complex transactions and provides built-in error handling.
  • Considerations: SOAP can be more complex to implement and maintain compared to REST-based services like OData.

When Not to Use OData

  • Performance Concerns: If the integration involves very high data volumes or requires complex transactions, OData might not be the best choice.
  • Legacy Systems: If the external system does not support RESTful services, other protocols like SOAP or IDoc might be more appropriate.

Long-term Sustainability

  • OData: Offers good extensibility and is easier to maintain due to its alignment with modern web standards.
  • IDoc/RFC/BAPI: These might require more specialized knowledge to maintain but are very stable for SAP-centric environments.

In conclusion, OData is often the preferred choice for modern, flexible integrations, especially when the backend is built using RAP/CDS. However, the choice of adapter should be based on specific project requirements, including performance, compatibility, and long-term maintenance considerations.

Kind regards,

Henrike

entry.title: Re: Operations not getting created from BAPI_ROUTING_CREA...
entry.content:
Hello,

Thank you very much for your question. I'm very sorry for the delay.

In this case, I asked an internal chatbot for support. Here's the response:

To resolve the issue where operations are not getting created when using the BAPI ‘BAPI_ROUTING_CREATE’, you should ensure that the data being passed is complete and correctly formatted. Here are a few steps to consider:

  1. Check Mandatory Fields: Ensure that all mandatory fields for operations are filled correctly. This includes fields like ACTIVITY, OPERATION_ID, CONTROL_KEY, WORK_CNTR, and PLANT.

  2. Sequence and Group Counter: Verify that the SEQUENCE_NO and GROUP_COUNTER are correctly formatted and match the expected format in the system. Leading zeros might be necessary depending on the system configuration.

  3. Data Consistency: Ensure that the data in lt_opr (operations table) is consistent with the data in lt_tsk (task list table) and lt_seq (sequence table). The TASK_LIST_GROUP and GROUP_COUNTER should match across these tables.

  4. Test in SE37: Since the data works in SE37, compare the data being passed in the BAPI call with the data used in SE37 to identify any discrepancies.

  5. Check for Errors: After the BAPI call, check the lt_return table for any error messages that might indicate why the operations are not being created.

  6. Transaction Commit: Ensure that the BAPI transaction is committed correctly using BAPI_TRANSACTION_COMMIT after the BAPI call.

I hope this helps. 

Kind regards,

Henrike

entry.title: Re: Looking for Reliable and Updated Practice Questions f...
entry.content:
Dear @petirklook,

Point 1. Verify the exam is the right one and still the one you’ll take

  • Use SAP Learning Get certified to locate your exact certification and open the related learning journey + exam entry
  • Also check SAP retirement rules in case an exam is being phased out
  • SAP is actively transitioning certifications to practical/performance-based formats in phases so the best source of truth is always what your Certification page shows right now

More details below:

https://learning.sap.com/get-certified

https://learning.sap.com/helpcenter/certification-support/certification-retirements

Point 2. Official learning content

These two official academies are explicitly positioned as preparation for C_TS4FI_2023:

  • TS4F01 Financial Accounting in SAP S/4HANA (Academy Part I)
  • TS4F02 Financial Accounting in SAP S/4HANA (Academy Part II)

For additional official courses you can mix in (e-learning / instructor-led) use SAP Training Financial Accounting training path as your catalog

More details below:

https://training.sap.com/trainingpath/Applications-Financial+Accounting-SAP+S4HANA

Point 3. Official SAP Learning Journeys

SAP certification flow explicitly points learners to Learning Journeys as the preparation path

More details below:

https://learning.sap.com/helpcenter/certification-support/how-to-get-certified

A relevant official journey to use as a structured checklist (FI core areas) is:

  • Implementing Financial Accounting in SAP S/4HANA Cloud

More details below:

https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/implementing-financial-accounting-in-sap-s-4hana?searchId=193f19e7-c1a5-4b2b-9ee5-bb315e1ccc21&listPosition=1

Point 4. Practice questions / mock tests what SAP officially provides

SAP generally does not publish a public official question bank for associate exams. The official alternatives are:

  • Hands-on practice systems bundled with certification offerings:
    • SAP Certification exam with includes of access to certification-relevant hands-on practice systems
  • SAP Learning Hub which includes hands-on practice systems and four certification exam attempts per year per subscription terms
  • Good book from SAP PRESS below:

https://www.sap-press.com/sap-s4hana-financial-accounting-certification-guide_5310/?srsltid=AfmBOooQzosm7vVH9RXnoufALO4iHdTZXsBszkXqXEV8RzUZtGmIU22i

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: From Business Objects to Business Flows: The Missing Layer of RAP Documentation
entry.content:
For documentation, we've been using arc42 (see arc42.org, if you're not familiar). Originally it is a structuring approach for documenting the architecture of entire systems, but I've found that it works quite nicely at all levels - the key questions and considerations apply (within reason) for artifacts of all sizes.

So you would e.g. have separate arc42 documents for:

  • the entire system
  • each of the main components and building blocks
  • any particularly complex bits and pieces within these building blocks
  • ...and so on (see the C4 model for inspiration).

At every level you would then document what is relevant for the current level of abstraction, including goals and constraints, solution approaches, static and flow diagrams, ADRs, and what have you. With hyperlinks, you can then tie the whole thing together, so that you can dive into the details when you need to, without being overwhelmed by mountains of text and large drawings that take hours to interpret without the proper context. The key is keeping text short and diagrams simple, so that they stay readable.

In your example, you could start with a document for the sales order process as a whole, and only document the high-level requirements and constraints, context, key building blocks, dependencies, high-level flows and related ADRs that are relevant at this level. For each building block (BO, or other substantial artefact) you would then create a separate document where you describe what is relevant for that particular object (pretty much the same topics as above, but from the perspective and with the level of detail relevant at this level).

Still, one documentation approach doesn't fit all requirements - business management needs different things than your fellow developers. But with this hierarchical structure you're pretty close, I find.

entry.title: How I Loaded 1.2 Billion Records into SAP HANA Without Crashing the Server (Or my Sanity)
entry.content:
Most SAP HANA performance discussions live in the comfortable world of millions of records. Benchmark slides, proof-of-concept demos, and architecture diagrams all look clean at that scale. But enterprise reality has a way of handing you something an order of magnitude larger and expecting the same SLA.

The Setup: When Scale Stops Being Theoretical

Most SAP HANA performance discussions live in the comfortable world of millions of records. Benchmark slides, proof-of-concept demos, and architecture diagrams all look clean at that scale. But enterprise reality has a way of handing you something an order of magnitude larger and expecting the same SLA.

In this case, the table in question was a virtual table — a HANA object that sits on top of a remote data source via Smart Data Access (SDA). The underlying dataset: 1.2 billion rows. The requirement: a daily delta load capturing changes since the last run, landing data into a local HANA columnar table for analytics consumption.

On paper, straightforward. In practice, the first execution ended in under two minutes — not because it was fast, but because it had already failed.

ERROR: Numeric value out of range: 2147483648 > 2147483647 (integer overflow)

If you've spent any time in SAP HANA internals, you recognize this immediately: a 32-bit integer overflow. The row count counter had exceeded the maximum value of a signed 32-bit integer — roughly 2.1 billion — when accounting for internal processing metadata. At 1.2 billion source records, certain HANA execution nodes were attempting to track intermediate results using data types that simply could not hold the numbers involved.

Diagnosing the Real Problem

The instinct in situations like this is to find a workaround — filter the dataset, process it in chunks, hope that the next HANA revision patches the behavior. We resisted that instinct because workarounds at this scale become permanent architecture decisions. The fix needed to be designed, not hacked.

The diagnostic phase revealed three compounding issues:

  • The virtual table had no physical partitioning. All query execution was being funneled through a single HANA node, creating both the overflow condition and a severe memory bottleneck.
  • The delta detection logic used a naive MAX(timestamp) comparison across the full source table. At 1.2 billion rows, even this baseline query was scanning far more data than necessary.
  • The target columnar table had been created with default settings, meaning no partition pruning was possible during write operations — every insert had to compete for the same table-level lock structures.

Any one of these would be a problem at scale. All three together made the load procedure functionally impossible without architectural changes.

The Architecture Decision: Hash-Based Distribution

The core decision was between range-based partitioning and hash-based distribution. Range partitioning is intuitive — split the data by date, by region, by some natural business boundary. But it creates a dangerous assumption: that your data arrives evenly across those boundaries. Delta loads rarely do. Business activity spikes, month-end processing creates surges, and range partitioning under uneven load creates hot partitions that defeat the purpose entirely.

Hash-based distribution solves this by distributing rows across partitions based on a hash of one or more key columns, regardless of value. The distribution is mathematically even by construction, not by assumption about your data. For a delta load pattern where the arrival distribution is unpredictable, this was the right choice.

Key insight: Hash distribution trades partition pruning capability (you can't easily skip partitions by value range) for guaranteed load distribution. For delta loads where you're always processing "new records since last run" rather than querying a specific range, this trade-off is almost always worth it.

The implementation used a composite key — a combination of the source system identifier and the record's primary key — as the hash input. This ensured that records from the same source system were spread across partitions rather than concentrated, which mattered because the source systems themselves had uneven record volumes.

The Procedure: Key Implementation Decisions

The delta load procedure was rebuilt from scratch with four core changes. I'll walk through the logic rather than exact client-specific code, but the patterns are directly replicable.

Step 1 — Establish the delta boundary without touching the full table:

-- Get last successful load timestamp from a control table

SELECT MAX(load_timestamp) INTO lv_last_load

FROM delta_control_table

WHERE load_status = 'SUCCESS';

This is a small but important detail. The previous implementation read the MAX timestamp from the source table itself — a full scan of 1.2 billion rows just to establish a starting point. Moving this to a dedicated control table eliminated what was effectively a full table scan before the actual work even began.

Step 2 — Partition the work at the procedure level, not just the table level:

-- Process in hash-distributed batches using modulo on source key

FOR i IN 0..7 DO

  INSERT INTO target_table

  SELECT * FROM source_virtual_table

  WHERE MOD(CAST(source_key AS BIGINT), 8)= i

  AND change_timestamp > lv_last_load;

END FOR;

The CAST to BIGINT here is the direct response to the original overflow. By explicitly casting the key to a 64-bit integer before the modulo operation, we moved the arithmetic out of the implicit 32-bit context that was causing the failure. The number 8 is the partition count — adjustable based on your HANA node count and available parallelism.

Step 3 — Explicit memory management between iterations:

-- Release intermediate results between batch iterations

EXEC 'UPDATE STATISTICS ON target_table';

Without this, HANA's query optimizer was working with stale statistics mid-procedure, leading to increasingly poor execution plans as the load progressed. Updating statistics between batches added modest execution time but dramatically improved consistency across the full load window.

The Results: Numbers That Justify the Redesign

Metric

Before Redesign

After Redesign

Load completion

Failed (overflow)

Consistent success

Execution time

N/A (failed)

~47 minutes avg

Peak memory per node

Unbounded (OOM)

~18 GB controlled

Delta detection scan

1.2B rows (full)

Control table only

Partition hot-spotting

Single node

Even 8-way distribution

 

What Generalizes: A Decision Framework for Your Next Billion-Record Load

If you take nothing else from this, take this decision tree. These are the questions I now ask before designing any large-scale HANA load procedure:

  • Is your row count approaching or exceeding 500 million? If yes, assume you will hit 32-bit arithmetic constraints somewhere in the execution path. Design BIGINT handling in explicitly from day one.
  • Is your delta arrival distribution predictable by a natural business key? If yes, range partitioning may work. If no — or if you're unsure — default to hash distribution.
  • Are you reading the delta boundary from your source table? Move this to a control table immediately. You are doing a full scan of your largest table to answer a question your database should already know.
  • Is your target table using default partition settings? For tables that will receive regular bulk inserts above 100 million rows, explicit partitioning is not optional — it is the difference between a load procedure and a production incident.

What I Would Do Differently

The honest answer is that the control table should have been part of the original design. Delta load architectures at any scale need a separate tracking mechanism — treating the source data as the source of truth for load state is a design flaw, not just a performance issue. At 1.2 billion records it becomes catastrophic, but it would have been wrong at 100 million too.

I would also have instrumented the procedure differently from the start. Adding execution time logging between each batch iteration, with row counts and memory snapshots, would have made the performance profiling much faster. We added this in a subsequent revision and it has saved significant debugging time on every load since.

 

Final Thoughts

SAP HANA is genuinely capable of handling data at this scale — but it requires you to work with its architecture rather than assuming it will absorb whatever you throw at it. The columnar engine, the distributed execution model, and the memory management system are all designed with specific usage patterns in mind. When you align your load procedures to those patterns, the performance characteristics are exceptional. When you don't, the failures are fast and unambiguous.

The 1.2 billion record delta load now runs reliably every night. It takes about 47 minutes, uses predictable memory, and has not failed since the redesign was deployed. That feels unremarkable today, which is exactly what good infrastructure architecture should feel like.

If you're working through a similar challenge, I'm happy to discuss in the comments. The specifics of hash key selection, batch count tuning, and control table schema design all depend on your environment — but the principles are consistent.

entry.title: Issue Filtering Empty Fields in OData Analytical Model Calls
entry.content:
Hello,

Over the past few weeks, we have been experiencing issues with OData calls to analytical models when applying filters on empty fields.

We are building the filter, for example, as follows:
ZOFFICE ne ' '

Until recently, this expression worked correctly. However, it is no longer excluding records with empty values (' '). Instead, it returns the full result set, including those records where the field is empty.

Has there been any recent change in how empty values are interpreted or handled in OData filters? We have also tried alternatives such as using null or comparison operators like gt, but none of them have produced the expected behavior.

Thank you,
Jaume

entry.title: AI-Assisted SAP Datasphere Modeling
entry.content:
The Evolution of SAP Datasphere Modeling

Creating and managing SAP Datasphere models has traditionally been a manual, time-consuming process. However, with the integration of AI tools like Trae IDE, this process has been revolutionized, allowing data teams to focus on business logic rather than technical implementation details.

The Benefits of AI-Assisted Modeling

Efficiency and Speed

  • Rapid Model Generation: Create complex models in minutes instead of hours
  • Batch Operations: Generate multiple models simultaneously
  • Error Reduction: Eliminate manual JSON formatting and syntax errors
  • Consistent Output: Maintain uniform model structures across projects

Intelligent Design

  • Context-Aware Modeling: AI considers your existing data landscape
  • Optimal Structures: Designs models based on best practices
  • Relationship Mapping: Automatically identifies and creates data relationships
  • Scalable Architecture: Builds models that grow with your business needs

Accessibility

  • Lower Learning Curve: Reduces the technical expertise required
  • Plain Language Input: Describe requirements in natural language
  • Self-Documenting: Generates well-documented model definitions
  • Team Collaboration: Bridges the gap between business and IT teams

How It Works with Trae IDE

Trae IDE, an AI-powered development environment, simplifies SAP Datasphere modeling through:

  1. Project Analysis: Trae analyzes your existing codebase and data structures
  2. Intelligent Suggestions: Provides context-aware recommendations
  3. Automated Generation: Creates complete JSON model definitions
  4. Seamless Deployment: Facilitates easy deployment to your SAP Datasphere tenant

A Real-World Example

Let's look at a practical example from my repository:

Creating a User Information Table

  1. Define Requirements (the prompt can be replaced with the design document in an advanced version)

    Create a user information table with ID, name, email, and creation date.
Deploy to the SAP_SCT space.

  2. Generated JSON Definition

    {
  "definitions": {
    "USER_INFO_TABLE": {
      "kind": "entity",
      "elements": {
        "USER_ID": {
          "@EndUserText.label": "User ID",
          "type": "cds.Integer",
          "key": true,
          "notNull": true
        },
        "USER_NAME": {
          "@EndUserText.label": "User Name",
          "type": "cds.String",
          "length": 100,
          "notNull": true
        },
        "EMAIL": {
          "@EndUserText.label": "Email",
          "type": "cds.String",
          "length": 200,
          "notNull": true
        },
        "CREATED_DATE": {
          "@EndUserText.label": "Created Date",
          "type": "cds.Date",
          "notNull": true
        }
      },
      "@EndUserText.label": "User Information Table"
    }
  }
}

  3. Deployment Command

    datasphere objects local-tables create --file-path user-info-table.json --space SAP_SCT --host https://dwc-field-training.eu10.hcs.cloud.sap

  4. Verification

    {
  "message": "Saved and deployed 'USER_INFO_TABLE' in 'SAP_SCT'"
}

Integration with Modern Workflows

This approach seamlessly fits into contemporary data engineering practices:

  • CI/CD Pipelines: Automate model deployments
  • Version Control: Track changes to model definitions
  • Documentation: Generate self-documenting models
  • Testing: Validate models before production deployment

Getting Started

To implement this approach in your projects:

  1. Set Up Your Environment

    • Install @sap/datasphere-cli
    • Configure authentication to your SAP Datasphere tenant
    • Set up Trae IDE for AI-assisted development

  2. Define Your Requirements

    • Clearly articulate data model needs
    • Provide examples of existing models
    • Specify target spaces and naming conventions

  3. Leverage AI Assistance

    • Use Trae IDE to generate model definitions
    • Review and refine generated JSON
    • Test deployment in a development environment

  4. Deploy and Iterate

    • Deploy models to production
    • Monitor performance and usage
    • Iterate based on feedback

Join the Conversation

This article presents just one approach to AI-assisted SAP Datasphere modeling. We invite you to explore this technique and share your experiences.

  • Project Repository: https://github.com/yuyonggang/dsp-model-creator
  • Discussion: Feel free to open issues or pull requests with your ideas and improvements

Conclusion

AI-assisted modeling represents a significant advancement in SAP Datasphere development. By leveraging tools like Trae IDE, data teams can focus on business value while reducing technical overhead.

This approach not only accelerates model creation but also improves quality and consistency across your data landscape. As AI technology continues to evolve, we can expect even more powerful capabilities to emerge, further transforming how we work with SAP Datasphere.

Whether you're a seasoned data engineer or new to SAP Datasphere, this approach offers a more efficient, reliable way to build and manage your data models.

entry.title: Gregor Wolf
entry.content:
About Gregor

  • Germany | SAP Mentor since 2007 | Follow Gregor on LinkedIn

I am an independent SAP solution architect, developer and SAP Mentor. I focus on SAP Business Technology Platform, SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP), Node.JS, SAP Fiori Elements and Security. I've supported several customers during their SAP Cloud ERP Private Edition (S/4HANA On Premise) implementation. Since the end of 2024 I support the technical topics at an SAP Cloud ERP Public Edition Customer.

Topics of interest: SAP Business Technology Platform, SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP), SAP Cloud ERP public and private, SAPUI5, OpenUI5, SAP Fiori, SAP HANA Cloud

Gregor, what inspired you to become an SAP Mentor?

A key influence early in my SAP Community journey was Mark Finnern. He is the father of the SAP Community. Mark encouraged me to move beyond answering questions and start sharing detailed technical how-to's but also event reports in blog posts. In 2007 Mark also founded the SAP Mentors program which gave me the opportunity to provide structured, experience-based feedback to SAP product teams and help bridge the gap between product vision and real-world implementation.

What advice would like to share with other SAP community members?

My advice to other SAP Community members is simple: contribute consistently and think long-term.

Start by answering questions and helping others solve real problems - but don’t stop there. Document your lessons learned, share architectural insights, and write about what worked and what didn’t. That’s how collective expertise grows. And you will be amazed how often you research years later and find your own answers and blog posts to solve a problem you're just facing. Despite all the new stuff SAP has quite a long tail that doesn't change quickly.

Engage constructively, challenge respectfully, and focus on signal over noise. The community is not just a support forum - it’s an ecosystem where practitioners can influence product direction, elevate standards, and shape the future of SAP together.

entry.title: Identifying and mitigating potential DDoS attacks in SAP Commerce Cloud
entry.content:
Introduction:

DDoS and DoS attacks are often a cause of headaches for SAP Commerce Cloud admins, causing slowness and at a times even disruptions to the operation of the site. In this article we will show how to identify and use standard tools to mitigate the problems caused by these attacks.

Symptom 

It's important to be aware of symptoms of DDoS attacks, some of them listed below are very commonly observed in such situations:

  1. Apache worker pool exhausted:


  2. Abnormal high throughput:


  3. Apache Tomcat Thread pool exhaustion:


  4. High amount of logs like: [client xx.xx.xxx.xx:xxxxx] AH01998: Connection closed to child 262 with abortive shutdown (server _:443)

Screenshots above taken by OOTB SAP - Load Test v2 Dynatrace dashboard.

Cause

There are different types of cyber attacks that could be causing instability on the environment. Most common scenarios seen with SAP Commerce Cloud customers are:

  • DDoS (Distributed Denial of Service: it uses multiple machines mostly known as "bots" to flood a target server with traffic)
  • DoS (Denial of Service: uses a single machine to flood a target server with traffic)

With the SAP Commerce Cloud overloaded with fake requests the pods start to crash because traffic is retained and pilled up in reverse proxy Apache Ingress, exhausting the worker pool and causing several side effects.

Identification of DDoS/DoS

To start is necessary to check if there are abnormal number of requests coming from a single IP. The approach below is specially useful when attackers are using automatic scan requests approach to flood the site requests. To do it you can go to Dynatrace failure rate increase alert page that will usually come with the DoS/DDoS symptoms:

Select the impacted web service:

Go to view dynamic requests:

Select Failure Rate tab, then go to the unusual request with a high failure rate, click on the three dots and Service backtrace:

Click on the endpoint and select Client IPs tab:

If an abnormal number of requests coming from a single unusual IP is observed, it can be further validated by using an online free IP Lookup tool to get more information on the suspicious IP. You can correlate to a DoS in case the address is from an unusual location or a malicious IP. You can even check Blacklist Status to validate if the address is listed on most common security services.
*Note that this is not an useful approach with setups using a CDN on top of their Storefront, as the original IP will be overwritten by CDN IP. In this case the one will need to verify at CDN level what are the suspicious IPs.

Mitigation 

Web Application Firewall Lite: In case a DoS attack is identified and there's no CDN on top of Storefront, enabling WAF lite in Cloud Portal should relief the traffic, specially the option "Allowed Open Connections from A Single IP", values should be reviewed for every particular case and can be changed to fit different scenarios. For more information, please refer to Basic Web Application Firewall. For installations using CDN, the WAF should be adjusted at CDN level, which should give more tailored firewall options.

IP Filter Sets: In case a DDoS attack is identified and there's no CDN on top of Storefront, you can make use of IP filter sets to block the source machines flooding traffic. For reference, please check IP Filter Sets documentation and 3203549 - How to allow / deny access of IP addresses based on geolocation - SAP Commerce Cloud.

What is NOT a DoS or DDoS attack: 

In various scenarios, similar symptoms may be observed, but it is crucial to recognize that these occurrences do not necessarily indicate a cyber attack. It is necessary to collaborate with the customer to verify whether there are any ongoing sales seasons, load tests or surge events affecting the environment. Additionally, a more in-depth assessment can be conducted using Dynatrace and OpenSearch to verify the legitimacy of incoming requests.

entry.title: POs are not transferred to accruals engine for SES accruals
entry.content:
Dear Members
I have configured Purchase Order Accruals (POAC) functionality in SAP S/4HANA 2023 version and is working fine. I also configured SES Accruals (SSAC). Raised a PO (ME21N) with account assignment category K and Item category D with a limit. PO date 01.02.2026 and the delivery date is 30.04.2026. I raised a service entry sheet via tcode ML81N with posting date 20.02.2026. When I run ACEPOSTINGRUN (with date 28.02.2026) this PO is not getting captured. 
My questions are

  2. If yes to the above, can I raise a SES via Fiori app F2027 against a PO raised via ME21N? 
Thanks
Kera

entry.title: Is there a dynamic form of "TYPES ... TYPE (structure) WITH INDICATORS ..."
entry.content:
I've got a code which is using "UPDATE zscarr FROM TABLE itab_zscarr INDICATORS SET STRUCTURE ind". ZSCARR is a copy of the table SCARR:

TYPES ty_zscarr TYPE zscarr WITH INDICATORS ind TYPE abap_bool.
DATA itab_zscarr TYPE TABLE OF ty_zscarr.
SELECT *
  FROM zscarr
	INTO CORRESPONDING FIELDS OF TABLE @itab_zscarr.
LOOP AT itab_zscarr REFERENCE INTO DATA(line_of_itab_zscarr).
  line_of_itab_zscarr->url = ''.
  line_of_itab_zscarr->ind-url = abap_true.
ENDLOOP.
UPDATE zscarr FROM TABLE itab_zscarr INDICATORS SET STRUCTURE ind.

The same code is to be done for other tables ZSCARR_2, ZSCARR_3... with the exact same structure.

I'd like to change the code to a dynamic form like this:

LOOP AT VALUE string_table( ( `ZSCARR` )
                            ( `ZSCARR_2` )
                            ( `ZSCARR_3` ) )
     INTO DATA(table_name).
  TYPES ty_zscarr TYPE (table_name) WITH INDICATORS ind TYPE abap_bool.
  DATA itab_zscarr TYPE REF TO DATA.
  CREATE DATA itab_zscarr TYPE TABLE OF (table_name).
  FIELD-SYMBOLS <itab_zscarr> TYPE TABLE.
  ASSIGN itab_zscarr->* TO <itab_zscarr>.
  SELECT *
    FROM (table_name)
    INTO CORRESPONDING FIELDS OF TABLE @<itab_zscarr>.
  LOOP AT <itab_zscarr> ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<line_of_itab_zscarr>).
    <line_of_itab_zscarr>-('URL') = ''.
    <line_of_itab_zscarr>-('IND-URL') = abap_true.
  ENDLOOP.
  UPDATE (table_name) FROM TABLE <itab_zscarr> INDICATORS SET STRUCTURE ind.
ENDLOOP.

All is fine except this obvious syntax error (TYPES has no dynamic form):

TYPES ty_zscarr TYPE (table_name) WITH INDICATORS ind TYPE abap_bool.

How to create the structured type WITH INDICATORS dynamically?

Thanks.

Sandra

NB: my system is S/4HANA 2023 FPS01 / ABAP 7.58 SP 1.

entry.title: Re: Is there a dynamic form of "TYPES ... TYPE (structure) WITH INDICATORS ..."
entry.content:
I propose below a helper class named ZCL_ABAP_RTTS to create a structured type WITH INDICATORS dynamically.

It compiles with ABAP 7.58 SP 1.

The class can be used as follows:

FIELD-SYMBOLS <itab_zscarr> TYPE STANDARD TABLE.

LOOP AT VALUE string_table( ( `ZSCARR` )
                            ( `ZSCARR_2` )
                            ( `ZSCARR_3` ) )
     INTO DATA(table_name).
  DATA(itab_zscarr) = zcl_abap_rtts=>get_table_with_indicators(
                            iv_structured_type_name      = table_name
                            iv_indicators_structure_name = 'IND' ##NO_TEXT
                            iv_indicator_type_char_1     = abap_true ).
  ASSIGN itab_zscarr->* TO <itab_zscarr>.

  SELECT *
    FROM (table_name)
    INTO CORRESPONDING FIELDS OF TABLE @<itab_zscarr>.

  LOOP AT <itab_zscarr> ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<line_of_itab_zscarr>).
    <line_of_itab_zscarr>-('URL') = ''.
    <line_of_itab_zscarr>-('IND-URL') = abap_true.
  ENDLOOP.

  UPDATE (table_name) FROM TABLE @<itab_zscarr> INDICATORS SET STRUCTURE ind.
ENDLOOP.

Code of class ZCL_ABAP_RTTS:

CLASS zcl_abap_rtts DEFINITION PUBLIC CREATE PRIVATE FINAL.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    CLASS-METHODS get_table_with_indicators
      IMPORTING structured_type_name         TYPE csequence
                indicators_component_name    TYPE csequence         DEFAULT 'IND'
                indicator_type_char_1        TYPE abap_bool         DEFAULT abap_false
                table_kind                   TYPE abap_tablekind    DEFAULT cl_abap_tabledescr=>tablekind_std
                unique_key                   TYPE abap_bool         DEFAULT abap_false
                user_keys                    TYPE abap_keydescr_tab OPTIONAL
                key_kind                     TYPE abap_keydefkind   DEFAULT cl_abap_tabledescr=>keydefkind_default
      RETURNING VALUE(table_with_indicators) TYPE REF TO data.

  PRIVATE SECTION.
    CLASS-METHODS get_structure_with_indicators
      IMPORTING structured_type_name             TYPE csequence
                indicators_component_name        TYPE csequence
                indicator_type_char_1            TYPE abap_bool DEFAULT abap_false
      RETURNING VALUE(structure_with_indicators) TYPE REF TO data.
ENDCLASS.


CLASS zcl_abap_rtts IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD get_structure_with_indicators.
    TYPES ty_ref_to_cl_abap_elemdescr TYPE REF TO cl_abap_elemdescr.

    " Get the types of the components of the input structure
    DATA(rtts_of_structured_type) = CAST cl_abap_structdescr( cl_abap_typedescr=>describe_by_name( structured_type_name ) ).
    DATA(components_with_indicators) = rtts_of_structured_type->get_components( ).

    " Create the indicators structure
    DATA(indicator_components) = VALUE cl_abap_structdescr=>component_table(
        LET rtts_indicator_component_type = COND ty_ref_to_cl_abap_elemdescr( WHEN indicator_type_char_1 = abap_false
                                                                              THEN cl_abap_elemdescr=>get_x( 1 )
                                                                              ELSE cl_abap_elemdescr=>get_c( 1 ) )
        IN
            FOR <ls_component> IN components_with_indicators
            ( name = <ls_component>-name
              type = rtts_indicator_component_type ) ).
    DATA(rtts_indicators_structure) = cl_abap_structdescr=>get( p_components = indicator_components
                                                                p_strict     = abap_false ).

    " Add the indicators structure to the final structure WITH INDICATORS, as one structured component
    INSERT VALUE cl_abap_structdescr=>component( name = EXACT #( indicators_component_name )
                                                 type = rtts_indicators_structure )
           INTO TABLE components_with_indicators.

    " Create the data object corresponding to the final structure WITH INDICATORS
    DATA(rtts_structure_with_indicators) = cl_abap_structdescr=>get( p_components = components_with_indicators
                                                                     p_strict     = abap_false ).
    CREATE DATA structure_with_indicators TYPE HANDLE rtts_structure_with_indicators.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD get_table_with_indicators.
    DATA(structure_with_indicators) = get_structure_with_indicators(
                                          structured_type_name      = structured_type_name
                                          indicators_component_name = indicators_component_name
                                          indicator_type_char_1     = indicator_type_char_1 ).
    DATA(rtts_table_with_indicators) = cl_abap_tabledescr=>get(
        p_line_type  = CAST #( cl_abap_typedescr=>describe_by_data_ref( structure_with_indicators ) )
        p_table_kind = table_kind
        p_unique     = unique_key
        p_key        = user_keys
        p_key_kind   = key_kind ).
    CREATE DATA table_with_indicators TYPE HANDLE rtts_table_with_indicators.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Verify Journal Entries submission screen not showing...
entry.content:
When submitting a General Journal Document, the document doesn't get posted but parked. Because from a business process logic, parking is supposed to serve as a functionality when you save the document to process it later. During this process, taxes are not calculated at all (also many other checks are not done) because the document can be incomplete.

Please refer to the below KBA for more information.

KBA - ##2857275 - No tax line in the App Verify General Journal Entries - F2728 

This is the standard behaviour and the system is working as designed.

entry.title: Re: 2FA for ABAP systems
entry.content:
Hello AJP

From https://me.sap.com/notes/3320671 

From the Service provider side (for example fiori), the ABAP does not support 2FA "out of the box" for the SAML2 SSO.
 However, in case the IDP itself supports 2FA, it can be implemented there.
From the SAP side, the only 2FA implementation that exists relies in the SAP SSO 3.0 TOTPLoginModule, which can be installed on AS Java systems (One-Time Password Authentication: https://help.sap.com/doc/5e008ecd41234762a9d5a9c33b0ad6fe/3.0/en-US/One-TimePasswordAuthentication_UACP.pdf), where the system may act as an IDP.

entry.title: SAP GUI Automation Failing to Recognize Screen – Possible Impact of Windows Idle Session Timeout
entry.content:
Hello Team,

We are currently experiencing an issue with one of our SAP GUI screen-based automations (SAP Build Process Automation / SAP GUI scripting).

The automation is able to:

  • Successfully log into Windows

  • Launch SAP GUI

  • Log into SAP

  • Navigate to the required transaction

  • Read data from SAP

However, after performing a period of background processing (Excel data handling), when the automation attempts to continue interacting with SAP GUI, it fails to recognize the expected screen.

At that moment, we observe the following popup message in SAP GUI:

“Idle timer expired. Session has been idle over its time limit. It will be disconnected in 2 minutes. Press any key now to continue session.”

Important observations:

  • SAP GUI does not immediately close.

  • The SAP Logon window remains open.

  • However, the automation is no longer able to recognize the screen elements.

  • The error typically appears as “Page not recognized” or “Element not found”.

We would like to confirm whether:

  1. A Windows Remote Desktop idle session timeout policy is active.

  2. Any recent security or infrastructure changes could be affecting GUI scripting sessions during periods of inactivity.

The automation is screen-based and depends on a stable SAP GUI session without modal popups or session interruptions.

Could you please confirm if the idle timeout policy (Windows or SAP level) may be impacting SAP GUI scripting and RPA execution?

Thank you in advance for your support.

Best regards,

entry.title: Re: “Stage” Button Not Triggering Any Action in Stage for...
entry.content:
Dear @kkumara,

1. Why Stage looks like it does nothing:

A. No popup is expected

In this proces Stage is not a wizard. It’s an action on the selected replenishment element(s). Document creation is part of the staging process and is typically finalized when you Save and not by opening a follow-up screen. SAP Note for the app explicitly talks about pressing Stage and whether the save becomes enabled / whether the delivery is created

SAP Note below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2960232

B. In your screenshot you already staged everything

Top table:

  • Miss. qty = 6 / 21
  • Quantity staged = 6 / 21
  • Remaining = 0.000 for both lines

Bottom table:

  • Replenishment elements exist
  • Quantity staged already filled

So the system considers the shortages fully staged already pressing Stage again produces no visible change often no message. This matches the typical staging logic

2. What documents should be created:

To verify creation:

  • Check Manage Outbound Deliveries (VL06O) / EWM monitoring as per SAP guidance delivery should be visible and linked to staging
  • If you expect a delivery but don’t see one even though the app log claims it was created, SAP has a dedicated Public Cloud Note for that symptom

SAP Notes below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3354265

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3624697

3. Control test:

Do this controlled test:

  • Pick a case where Remaining > 0 a real shortage not yet staged or create a new requirement
  • In the Pull List select the bottom table (Replen. Elements) line(s) next Stage and Save
  • Then verify delivery creation in the delivery monitoring apps above

SAP Note below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2960232

Summary:

  • Nothing happens because there is nothing left to stage quantity staged fully covers Missing qty and Remaining is 0

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: SAP Tax and Revenue Management - What is new for the S/4HANA 2025 FPS1 Release
entry.content:
Dear readers,

In a previous blog (SAP Tax and Revenue Management - What is new for the S/4HANA 2025 (initial) release I covered the new developments introduced in the S/4HANA 2025 FPS0 release. In this post, I will discuss what’s new in the S/4HANA 2025 FPS1 release:

  • Enable archiving of form data stored in the business document service (BDS/XML)

For Tax & Revenue Management, the archiving for form bundles was enhanced by the possibility to archive XML form data during the archiving run and delete the XML forms during the deletion run.

Compressed XML forms and alternative XML storage are supported as well.

The existing reload program was enhanced to handle reloading/re-creation of BDS documents for XML forms and restoring the DFMCA_FORM_DATA entries for XML forms.

This development has been downloaded all the way to ECC EhP8 (https://me.sap.com/notes/3648380)

For more information, see:

 

  • Disable the creation of new data form versions when updating existing form data

In legacy TRM, every change of formdata of an XML form lead to the creation of a new data version (corresponding to an extra line in dfmca_form_vers and an extra internal XML document).  This new development allows the prevention of the creation of new data versions via customizing.

The new development is relevant for all channels processing forms: standard UI processes, API usage (via FMCA_RETURN_UPDATE), or “upload” via Emigall (via FMCA_RETURN_UPDATE).

For more information, see:

 

  • Virtual Data Model for Tax and Revenue Management analytics

Virtual data model for analytics in tax and revenue management that provides streamlined and harmonized access to the tax and revenue management data model

Content that includes form bundle headers and form data for tax and revenue management analytics

For more information, see:

entry.title: Re: On production order release, two storage locations st...
entry.content:
Dear @SANDY13973,

In Public Cloud standard ATP/material availability check can be either:

  • Storage-location level checks only the issue/production SLoc in the requirement

or

  • Plant level checks across storage locations in the plant

The switch is in Configure Scope of Availability Check in CBC as SSCUI 101099

SAP Note below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3246520

 

Indicator: Without Storage Location Check

  • Set: ATP check is performed at plant level (so stock from multiple SLocs is included)
  • Not set: Only the production/issue storage location is considered

So the standard way to consider 2 SLoc stocks is to switch to plant-level check

IMPORTANT: Standard configuration does not provide sum stock from exactly two specific SLocs but exclude others. Plant-level means all storage locations of the plant are in scope. If you truly must limit to exactly 2 you typically solve it operationally for example keep stock only in those SLocs for that material or consolidate into a staging SLoc before release

To release production order only if components are available use option in CBC Define Checking Control SSCUI 102447. SAP confirms there are 3 release options when parts are missing:

 

1. User decides on release if parts are missing

2. Release permitted despite missing parts

3. No release if parts are missing

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3516522

https://learning.sap.com/courses/configuring-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition-manufacturing-execution/configuring-material-availability-checks-for-production-orders

If you want the system to block release, use option 3 for your plant + order type entry

IMPORTANT: If you use background/mass processes/APIs the User decides … popup option is not handled because no dialog can be shown. SAP explicitly notes only option 2 & 3 are considered in API context

SAP Note below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3029979

Additional information:

Public Cloud uses Fiori apps/background jobs but the same SSCUI 102447 logic applies for collective release. SAP learning explicitly references collective release via apps like Manage Production Orders / Mass Processing Production Orders and background release via Schedule Order Release Run

More details below:

https://learning.sap.com/courses/implementing-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition-manufacturing/releasing-production-orders-1

For mass availability checking you can also schedule background checks via Schedule Collective Availability Check for Orders

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/2bba750d1e124e1ea2a039bb1cd9b6c5/473277665900410b9e92ef9861ebb748.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/2bba750d1e124e1ea2a039bb1cd9b6c5/c21cf9fb0c514422a3875a96e9f74352.html

In CBC in SSCUI 102447 ensure you maintain:

  • the correct Plant + Order Type
  • the correct Checking Rule this checking rule + the material’s checking group determines which scope of check entry is used
  • and also align the Material availability check behavior at release for example Status check vs Check when saving to ensure missing parts status is correctly enforced during release.

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3686314

https://learning.sap.com/courses/configuring-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition-manufacturing-execution/configuring-material-availability-checks-for-production-orders

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Pricing in Service PO according to service specification
entry.content:
In SAP S/4HANA, I am creating a Service PO (item category D) using a generic service material for example "Cutting".
The actual service price depends on specific service attributes (for example: thickness range, width range, weight bracket, grade, etc.).

If I maintain yearly contracts with pricing in SAP, the PO can pull the net price from the contract item.
However, for detailed pricing (e.g., multiple price components), I normally need to enter lines in the Service tab (ESLL) manually.

My question is:
Is there any SAP standard way for the system to automatically determine the correct service price and automatically populate the Service tab (ESLL) based on service attributes, without manual entry?

Specifically:

  • How can SAP “know” which price line applies?
  • Can service specifications drive automatic pricing in standard?
  • Is contract pricing with scales or conditions the only standard option?

entry.title: Re: More insights, faster resolution: Expanding Base Memory for SAP Cloud ALM
entry.content:
Hello, I can give an example from the Implementation perspective. If you have more than one SAP Cloud ALM system, important data ends up distributed in multiple places which limits your ability to re-use the data (for example, solution documentation, tasks, requirements, test cases, etc.) In a single SAP Cloud ALM, you can assign a document to multiple entities such as the process hierarchy, process diagrams, tasks, requirements, and more. However, you cannot easily browse, find and then assign objects from a different SAP Cloud ALM. It's possible to assign a document located in another SAP Cloud ALM by URL reference, but this only displays the header data and limits how it's searchable from another tenant. 

SAP is investigating methods of sharing information between SAP Cloud ALM tenants, but there is no details yet on availability. This question came up previously in the SAP Community post: How can we get one cloud alm tenant data into another cloud alm tenant and using which api? 

There is an exception in the case of Service Delivery information - customers now have the option to maintain their own mapping which service information should go into which SAP Cloud ALM tenant. See the SAP Community post: Transfer of Service Data for Customers entitled to several SAP Cloud ALM Tenants.

entry.title: Re: Determinations in ABAP RESTful Programming Model
entry.content: Hi,  trigger conditions can be only modify operations (create, update, delete) CUD. Read operations do not invoke a determination.

entry.title: IBP Phase Out Issues - moving unwanted volume
entry.content:
We have Phase In for new products working well, but have not perfected Phase Out yet.  Example:

Product Group A has 5 Material IDs with the following shipment history:

Material IDHistorical Month 1Historical Month 2Historical Month 3Historical Month 4Historical Month 5Historical Month 6
Item 11121001039811294
Item 2839491889987
Item 3161169171150154178
Item 4257742
Item 59910611594103115

If we decide products 1,2,3, and 5 are phasing out and put end date in before the 4th item will phase out, the forecast aggregation sends ALL the remaining volume in item 4, as shown below.  This remaining item is not actually expected to increase, the volume will be moving to a different product family.  

Material IDForecast Month 1Forecast Month 2Forecast Month 3Forecast Month 4Forecast Month 5Forecast Month 6
Item 110700000
Item 2000000
Item 31641720000
Item 4199273448450460475
Item 5000000

Is there a way to stop the future volume from being shifted?  Or a way to mark a material so its volume is not referenced in the future for forecasting purposes?

Any ideas are greatly appreciated!

entry.title: Breaking SAP Data Barriers with Datasphere & Databricks: A Medallion Journey (Bronze, Silver, Gold)
entry.content:
IMPORTANT NOTE

SAP Datasphere does not replace Medallion architecture.
It simply shifts or enhances parts of your pipeline.

Think of it like this:

Datasphere = SAP-centric modeling + governed data access
Databricks = Open lakehouse + scalable transforms + AI/ML engine

Together, they complement each other.

Understanding the Medallion Architecture for SAP Data Layers

The Medallion approach structures data into layers, improving data quality as it moves upward.

🥉Bronze Layer – Raw SAP Data Landing Zone

This is where SAP data enters into Datasphere with Databricks.

What’s inside:

  • Integrated Raw data extracted from SAP ECC/S4 (tables, CDS views, BW objects)
  • Data in SAP-specific formats such as IDocs, ABAP extracts, or ODP streams
  • No transformations—just ingestion in its original state

Sources commonly used:

  • SAP ODP (Operational Data Provisioning)
  • SAP SLT (real‑time replication)
  • SAP BW extractors
  • SAP S/4HANA CDS Views
  • Files from SAP Application Servers

Goals of Bronze:

  • Ensure complete, unaltered SAP data
  • Maintain lineage to SAP tables
  • Enable incremental loading (Delta)
  • Provide a historical snapshot

Think of Bronze as your foundation of truth.

🥈Silver Layer – Cleaned & Harmonized SAP Data

This layer transforms raw SAP data into analytics‑ready tables.

What happens in Silver:

  • Data cleansing (null handling, type casting, deduplication)
  • SAP business logic harmonization
  • Joining related tables (e.g., EKKO + EKPO for Purchasing)
  • Enriching with master data (Material, Customer, Vendor, GL Accounts)
  • Converting SAP-specific formats (like NUMC, timestamps, currencies)

Results:

You get clean, standardized, and reusable data models.

Why Silver matters:

Example : SAP ERP tables are highly normalized and cryptic—full of codes like MATNR, WERKS, EBELN.
Silver makes them meaningful for downstream analytics.

🥇Gold Layer – Business-Ready SAP Analytics

This top layer contains curated, business-oriented datasets.

Examples:

  • Financial dashboards (GL, AR, AP)
  • CO-PA profitability models
  • Supply chain KPI models (OTIF, Inventory Aging)
  • Production analytics (yield, scrap, downtime)
  • Procurement insights (Spend Analytics)

Gold = Business Value

Here data is aggregated, enriched, and structured for:

  • BI tools (BW4, SAP Analytics Cloud)
  • ML models (forecasting, optimizations)
  • AI agents

It’s optimized for consumption, not technical storage.

The Medallion Architecture—Bronze, Silver, Gold—is the backbone for unleashing SAP data on Databricks.


LayerPurposeExamples
BronzeRaw SAP dataTables, IDocs, ODP extracts
SilverCleaned, harmonized SAP dataJoined purchasing docs, material data
GoldBusiness insightsFinance, supply chain, sales, procurement

 

Does Medallion Architecture Work with SAP Datasphere?

Yes — but it depends on how you use Datasphere.

The Medallion Architecture (Bronze → Silver → Gold) is a Databricks data engineering pattern.
SAP Datasphere is SAP’s cloud data fabric, which also has its own modeling layers (staging, semantic models, analytical models).

When Databricks and SAP Datasphere are connected, the Medallion Architecture still applies — but the roles can shift depending on your strategy.

Side-by-Side Comparison

ComponentSAP Datasphere RoleSAP Databricks Medallion Role
Raw data ingestionODP, SLT, SAP modelsBronze
Data cleansing & harmonizationOptional (Datasphere models)Silver
Business logic, KPIsOptional (Analytical models)Gold
Advanced AI/MLLimitedDatabricks ML/AI workspace
Scalable storageProprietary (SAP HANA Cloud)Open storage (Delta Lake)
